core_behavior: The system must authenticate users quickly and securely, allowing them to access the application or service.
The login system will verify the user's credentials (username and password) against a stored database of user information.
If the credentials are valid, the system will grant access to the user.
inputs: Username: a string of characters (alphanumeric, special characters) with a minimum length of 3 characters and a maximum length of 50 characters.
Password: a string of characters (alphanumeric, special characters) with a minimum length of 8 characters and a maximum length of 128 characters.
Optional: two-factor authentication (2FA) code, which is a numeric or alphanumeric code sent to the user's registered email or phone number.
outputs: Success: Upon successful authentication, the system will return a JSON Web Token (JWT) or a session ID, which will be used to authenticate subsequent requests.
Failure: If the authentication fails, the system will return an error message indicating the reason for the failure (e.g., "Invalid username or password").
assumptions: The system will use a relational database management system (e.g., MySQL) to store user information.
The system will use a hashing algorithm (e.g., bcrypt) to store passwords securely.
The system will use a secure communication protocol (e.g., HTTPS) to encrypt data transmitted between the client and server.
The system will have a rate limiting mechanism to prevent brute-force attacks.
The system will use a caching mechanism (e.g., Redis) to improve performance.
failure_modes: If the user enters an invalid username or password, the system will return an error message and increment a failed login attempt counter. After a specified number of failed attempts (e.g., 5), the system will temporarily lock out the user's account.
If the system encounters a database error or connection issue, it will return a generic error message and log the error for further investigation.
If the system detects a potential security threat (e.g., a brute-force attack), it will trigger an alert and notify the system administrator.
edge_cases: Empty or null input: The system will return an error message if the username or password is empty or null.
Special characters: The system will accept special characters in the username and password, but it will also perform input validation to prevent SQL injection attacks.
Non-ASCII characters: The system will accept non-ASCII characters in the username and password, but it will also perform input validation to prevent encoding attacks.
Concurrent logins: The system will allow multiple concurrent logins from the same user, but it will also monitor and prevent suspicious activity.
security_constraints: The system must comply with the OWASP security guidelines and best practices.
The system must use secure password storage and transmission protocols.
The system must implement rate limiting and IP blocking to prevent brute-force attacks.
The system must use secure communication protocols (e.g., HTTPS) to encrypt data transmitted between the client and server.
performance_constraints: The system must respond to login requests within 500ms (average response time).
The system must be able to handle a minimum of 100 concurrent login requests per second.
The system must have a minimum uptime of 99.9% and be able to recover from failures within 5 minutes.
The system must use caching and indexing to improve query performance and reduce database load.
